analysis.overall_assessment: The image shows a single, unhealthy leaf with brown spots on it. The rest of the plant appears to be healthy.

primary_pollution_source: Acid rain or excess nitrogen fertilizers
pollution_reduction_solutions: Reduce the use of chemical fertilizers and switch to organic alternatives
Implement proper drainage systems to prevent acid rain damage

processing_recommendations.sorting_strategy: Separate healthy and unhealthy leaves during sorting
processing_recommendations.processing_method: Process healthy leaves separately to prevent contamination
quality_preservation: Ensure proper washing of all tea leaves before processing

waste_prevention.salvageable_portions: Healthy leaves can still be used for processing
alternative_uses: Use burnt or browned tips as a natural sweetener in recipes
waste_prevention.composting_guidelines: Compost unhealthy leaves separately from healthy ones to prevent contamination
prevention_measures: Monitor soil pH levels and adjust accordingly to prevent acid rain damage

economic_impact.estimated_loss_percentage: 1% loss in overall tea leaf quality
cost_saving_opportunities: Invest in soil testing and pH adjustment to prevent acid rain damage
value_recovery_methods: Sell burnt or browned tips as a natural sweetener

priority_actions: Reduce chemical fertilizer use and switch to organic alternatives
recommendations.timeline: Immediate actions can be taken within 1 month, long-term improvements should be made over the next 6 months
monitoring_points: Regularly test soil pH levels and adjust as needed
Monitor leaf health during processing to prevent contamination
